findings.title: Updater signatures are replayable across deployments that reuse the same domain and updater key

best_agent_match.title: Updater signatures are replayable across deployments that reuse the same domain and updater key

findings.title: Replica silently truncates 32-byte recipients to 20 bytes and dispatches to non-contract addresses

best_agent_match.title: Replica truncates 32-byte recipients to 20 bytes and dispatches without validating code exists

rejected_candidates.title: The documented `Failed` state does not actually stop updates or message execution
rejected_candidates.reason: The `state` variable is indeed unused, but this codebase exposes no reachable path that ever sets `state = Failed`; in the current implementation it is dead code/comment drift rather than an exploitable protocol flaw.

rejected_candidates.title: Updater rotation can permanently orphan already-signed but unrelayed roots
rejected_candidates.reason: Changing `updater` invalidates old signatures, but the new updater can re-sign the same root transition or any later append-only root, so the code does not itself permanently strand messages.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Origin Domain Verification Allows Arbitrary Cross-Domain Message Injection
rejected_candidates.reason: The message `origin` field is authenticated by Merkle inclusion in an updater-signed root for the followed Home; lack of a separate `origin == remoteDomain` check is not a standalone forgery vector.

rejected_candidates.title: prove() Allows Re-proving Messages Under New Roots Without Validation
rejected_candidates.reason: Re-proving an unprocessed message under another acceptable root is intentional append-only behavior and does not bypass the processed check or create capabilities beyond whatever roots are already trusted.

rejected_candidates.title: Governance Can Set Arbitrary Roots Bypassing Security Controls
rejected_candidates.reason: `setConfirmation` is an explicit owner-only emergency override; a malicious or compromised governance owner is a trust assumption, not a distinct implementation bug.

rejected_candidates.title: Single Updater Key Creates Centralized Single Point of Failure
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a protocol trust-model observation rather than a code vulnerability; the contracts are explicitly designed around a trusted updater role.

rejected_candidates.title: Optimistic Timeout Can Be Set To Zero During Initialization
rejected_candidates.reason: The lack of a minimum on first initialization is an explicit deployment/configuration choice for testing/bootstrap scenarios and requires trusted deployment misconfiguration, not a permissionless exploit.

rejected_candidates.title: No Protection Against Proof Reuse After Message Processing
rejected_candidates.reason: Processed messages cannot be reproven because `prove()` rejects `LEGACY_STATUS_PROCESSED`; reproving before processing is the same intended behavior already covered by the other re-proving candidate and is not independently exploitable.

rejected_candidates.title: process() Does Not Check Return Value of handle() External Call
rejected_candidates.reason: `IMessageRecipient.handle` has no return value; if the external call reverts, the whole transaction reverts and the processed status/event are rolled back, so there is no false-success path from an ignored return value.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Zero Address Check for Updater
rejected_candidates.reason: Setting `updater` to `address(0)` is recoverable by the owner via a later `setUpdater` call, so it does not permanently brick the bridge and is not a protocol-level vulnerability.
